rationale: This is a canonical example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, featuring the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich phenol (resorcinol) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (zinc chloride) and a Brønsted acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone (2,4-dihydroxyacetophenone) after hydrolysis of the intermediate ketimine. This example clearly illustrates the key transformation: the formation of a C(aryl)-C(acyl) bond from an arene and a nitrile.
collision_avoidance_note: The reaction uses a simple, highly activated phenol (resorcinol) and a simple nitrile (acetonitrile), which are characteristic reactants for this specific named reaction. This avoids confusion with the more general Friedel-Crafts acylation, which typically uses acyl halides or anhydrides. The use of a nitrile as the acylating agent is the defining feature of the Houben-Hoesch reaction. The simple, acyclic nature of the nitrile and the specific substitution pattern of the product help to distinguish it from other electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.
